title: Connectivity issue for rootless qbittorrent container using pasta driver
body:
### Issue Description

It seems that qbittorrent running rootless via the default pasta driver (specifically if launched via podman compose or a quadlet) has trouble acquiring an external IP, which breaks DHT connectivity. Reverting to the `slirp4netns` driver fixes the issue for compose/quadlet invocations, and running qbittorrent via command-line using otherwise identical arguments also seems to work OK.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. After starting the qbittorrent container, navigate to web UI, then enable Logs in View menu. Click on Logs tab and check output for any line(s) matching `Detected external IP: xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx`. Additionally, watch the DHT node count at bottom of the web UI.

2. For testing the command-line invocation:
```
podman run -d \
  --name=qbittorrent \
  -e PUID=1000 \
  -e PGID=1000 \
  -e TZ=Etc/UTC \
  -e WEBUI_PORT=8080 \
  -e TORRENTING_PORT=6881 \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -p 6881:6881 \
  -p 6881:6881/udp \
  -v ~/docker/qbittorrent/config:/config \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
```

2. Compose definition (same effect if converted to a  quadlet):
```
services:
  qbittorrent:
    image: lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
    container_name: qbittorrent
    environment:
      - PUID=1000
      - PGID=1000
      - TZ=Etc/UTC
      - WEBUI_PORT=8080
      - TORRENTING_PORT=6881
    volumes:
      - ~/docker/qbittorrent/config:/config
    ports:
      - 8080:8080
      - 6881:6881
      - 6881:6881/udp
    restart: unless-stopped
```


### Describe the results you received

When launched via command-line, external IP is resolved and DHT count will increase as normal. The pasta process will show these arguments:
```
/usr/bin/pasta --config-net -t 6881-6881:6881-6881 -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 -u 6881-6881:6881-6881 --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-5f81f8cc-b7b7-c851-af04-c24e80a4fddb
```

When launched via quadlet or compose with the default pasta driver, the external IP is not resolved in 95% of cases, and DHT count remains at 0. In some rare cases, the external IP is resolved after an extended delay, but the DHT count does not increase at the expected rate. Pasta process will show:
```
/usr/bin/pasta --config-net --pid /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns
```

When launched via quadlet/compose and `default_rootless_network_cmd` is set to `slirp4netns`, external IP resolution and DHT works correctly at all times.


### Describe the results you expected

External IP and DHT connectivity should be working in all configurations.


### podman info output

```yaml
$ podman info

  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.37.2

  cpuUtilization:

    uidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000

    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
  kernel: 6.10.6-zen1-1-zen
  linkmode: dynamic
  logDriver: journald
  memFree: 684138496
  memTotal: 7173124096
  networkBackend: netavark
  networkBackendInfo:
    backend: netavark
    dns:
      package: aardvark-dns-1.12.1-1

      version: aardvark-dns 1.12.1
    package: netavark-1.12.2-1

    version: netavark 1.12.2
  ociRuntime:
    name: crun

    version: |-

      rundir: /run/user/1000/crun
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +YAJL
  os: linux
  pasta:
    executable: /usr/bin/pasta
    package: passt-2024_08_21.1d6142f-1

```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Arch Linux x86_64.

### Additional information

I can reproduce the exact same results with the same podman/pasta version on Arch Linux ARM aarch64 running on a Raspberry Pi 4.

comments.body:
> When launched via quadlet or compose with the default pasta driver [...] Pasta process will show:
> 
> ```
> /usr/bin/pasta --config-net --pid /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns
> ```

For some reason, ports are not forwarded at all (you get `-t 6881-6881:6881-6881 -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 -u 6881-6881:6881-6881 -T none -U none` when started by command line).

> > When launched via quadlet or compose with the default pasta driver [...] Pasta process will show:
> > ```
> > /usr/bin/pasta --config-net --pid /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns
> > ```
> 
> For some reason, ports are not forwarded at all (you get `-t 6881-6881:6881-6881 -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 -u 6881-6881:6881-6881 -T none -U none` when started by command line).

Yes, I noticed this discrepancy, but I'm not certain how pasta is supposed to operate in bridged mode (which seems to be the main networking difference between quadlet/compose vs command line invocation).

I tested the following:
1. `pasta_options = ["-t", "6881-6881:6881-6881", "-t", "8080-8080:8080-8080", "-u", "6881-6881:6881-6881"]` 
This results in the following error:
```
Error: rootlessport listen tcp 0.0.0.0:6881: bind: address already in use
Error: unable to start container "a1dcbf164f52ced90452666254b5d00005ce238e940fc1d228acf5b04034910d": rootlessport listen tcp 0.0.0.0:6881: bind: address already in use
```
2. `pasta_options = ["-t", "auto", "-u", "auto"]`
Process spawns with the following arguments, but unfortunately does not improve the issue:
```
conn        4716  0.2  0.2  71932 15508 ?        Ss   15:24   0:00 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net -t auto -u auto --pid /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns
```

The bridge mode is special and never uses pasta for port forwarding, i.e. see https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/22943#discussioncomment-9795883.
But slirp4netns works in the same way so I do not see how this would cause any of this, i.e. port forwarding source ip is wrong (https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8193).

How does qbittorrent actually try to figure out its external ip address?

I'm not well-versed on qBittorrent development, but my hunch is that it retrieves the ip address through libtorrent. So it would likely be [this](https://github.com/qbittorrent/qBittorrent/blob/72feee6fddc199d8cd49e7de4c35e080cbc353a5/src/base/bittorrent/sessionimpl.cpp#L5888-L5900) function which calls [external_address()](https://github.com/arvidn/libtorrent/blob/b1fd3ed5fd305f073ab443f39622f3e10d7bc0af/src/session_impl.cpp#L6954-L6970) in libtorrent.

Although my test case doesn't apply (as I didn't set up an IPv6-enabled nework in the compose definition), after further testing I can see that rootless bridge mode has an issue resolving my IPv6 address even with `slirp4netns`. 

In summary:
* When `network_mode` is set to `pasta` or `slirp4netns` in the compose/quadlet definition, the external IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are retrieved correctly.
* Rootless bridge with pasta driver cannot resolve any external addresses (5% of the time, an IPv4 address resolves after a delay, but DHT is still degraded)
* Rootless bridge with slirp4netns driver only resolves the IPv4 external address.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I have the same issue but for user created networks only. The default podman network works as expected. From what I can see pasta doesn't get the correct portmappings.
```

# default podman network
ps aux |grep -i pasta
user       3410  0.0  0.2  71940 12668 ?        Ss   10:18   0:00 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net -u 51413-51413:51413-51413 -t 9091-9091:9091-9091 -t 51413-51413:51413-51413 --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-1217136f-ebce-13d5-bb4c-ff59950580bd

# user created network
ps aux |grep -i pasta
user       4108  0.5  0.2  71936 12648 ?        Ss   10:43   0:00 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net --pid /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid --dns-forward 169.254.0.1 -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns
```

When i switch to slirp4netns it works like a charm.
user defined networks do not use pasta or slirp4netns for port forwarding, they use rootlessport: https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/22943#discussioncomment-9795883
Thank you for the feedback. It is odd to me that changing to slirp4netns would make a difference if it's bypassed?

> It is odd to me that changing to slirp4netns would make a difference if it's bypassed?

It's probably due to a difference in the address that's reported to qbittorrent, see https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23739#issuecomment-2310186061. I'm not sure how that affects things, more investigation is definitely needed.

To clarify I use another bittorrent client called [transmission](https://github.com/transmission/transmission). I don't know if it uses the same libraries or not. Anyway I would be really happy if this issue could get resolved and I will gladly help out if you need more information.

> To clarify I use another bittorrent client called [transmission](https://github.com/transmission/transmission). I don't know if it uses the same libraries or not. Anyway I would be really happy if this issue could get resolved and I will gladly help out if you need more information.

What would help here is some slightly deeper analysis of the issue (there's a start of it in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23739#issuecomment-2310186061): what do qbittorrent and Transmission expect to find as container address? How does the fact that slirp4netns does NAT, and the fact that pasta (by default) doesn't affect this? What happens if you specify options for pasta so that it behaves roughly like slirp4netns (look for `pasta` in `man podman-run`)? Which ones are actually needed?

OpenSUSE MicroOS is the os

I'm having the same issue with the message "Dropping datagram with no flow TAP..." and it makes the pasta process consuming 95% of a core. In my case, I have IPv4 and IPv6 configured and in the log appears IPv6 IPs:

```
jun 02 17:45:14 archy pasta[149944]: Dropping datagram with no flow TAP 2a0c:...:6771 -> ff15::efc0:988f:6771
```

I have created the container rootless with:
```
podman run -p 8080:8080 -p 6881:6881 -p 6881:6881/udp -v /home/servarr/qbittorrent/config:/config -v /home/disco4tb:/media -v /home/yuki/Descargas/torrents:/torrents --userns=keep-id lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
```

and I see the pasta process as:
```
 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net -t 6881-6881:6881-6881 -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 -u 6881-6881:6881-6881 --dns-forward 169.254.1.1 -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-c32d10d6-ccea-75c3-b6db-388198fff650 --map-guest-addr 169.254.1.2
```

With top or htop I see the process consuming a 95% of CPU. If I pause the downloaded files, the process downs to consume 0,6%-1%... I assume that the process consumes more  CPU because it needs to drop all those packages, because when I restart the download goes up the consumption.

Let me know if you need more info.

In the Qbittorrent logs I see that it detects external IPs.

> I'm having the same issue with the message "Dropping datagram with no flow TAP..." and it makes the pasta process consuming 95% of a core. In my case, I have IPv4 and IPv6 configured and in the log appears IPv6 IPs:
> 
> ```
> jun 02 17:45:14 archy pasta[149944]: Dropping datagram with no flow TAP 2a0c:...:6771 -> ff15::efc0:988f:6771
> ```

@dgibson any idea about any recent change that might cause this? That ff15:: multicast address looks weird to me but we should probably still establish a flow for those.

What I've seen is that port 6771 is bittorrent's "local peer discovery service". It can be disabled in Qbittorrent, but by default is  enabled.

I have seen also:

```
ss -pnta | grep -i 6771
LISTEN     0      128             *:6771              *:*     users:(("passt.avx2",pid=185886,fd=153))
```

Not sure if with this I should get "pasta" or it's ok. 

I have updated the Qbittorrent image (lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent) and all the services that I have running, and now I'm also having IPv4 drops in the logs

```
jun 03 20:56:57 archy pasta[202587]: Dropping datagram with no flow TAP 2a0c:5a86:2107:d600:6021:e1ff:febd:2a16:6771 -> ff15::efc0:988f:6771
jun 03 20:56:57 archy pasta[202587]: Dropping datagram with no flow TAP 2a0c:5a86:2107:d600:6021:e1ff:febd:2a16:6771 -> ff15::efc0:988f:6771
jun 03 20:57:00 archy pasta[202587]: Can't process IPv4 fragments (13 dropped)
jun 03 20:57:00 archy pasta[202587]: Can't process IPv4 fragments (14 dropped)
jun 03 20:57:01 archy pasta[202587]: Dropping datagram with no flow TAP 2a0c:5a86:2107:d600:6021:e1ff:febd:2a16:6771 -> ff15::efc0:988f:6771
jun 03 20:57:01 archy pasta[202587]: Dropping datagram with no flow TAP 2a0c:5a86:2107:d600:6021:e1ff:febd:2a16:6771 -> ff15::efc0:988f:6771
```

I can't immediately think of a change that would cause this problem.  But here are my observations so far:

- I think there's a good chance the `Dropping datagram` error is the cause of the other address problems - we're dropping the packets that qtbittorrent is using to do its external address discovery
- It's not really clear whether it's logically correct to create a flow (or at least the normal type of flow) for multicast packets.  One of the main points of the flow is so we can associate replies with the originating packets, but for multicast replies are not just a matter of switching source/dest addresses, so the existing flow logic isn't really correct.
- That said, creating a flow is probably the quickest way to something working.
- There is one place we explicitly require a unicast addresses, but I don't think that should be in play - it should only trigger if we saw a packet with a multicast *source* address, here it's the destination.

Next things to check:
- Can we get a full log, or at least a section leading up to the `Dropping datagram` message.  There might be other messages giving more detail about why we're failing.
- Especially if we could enable debug or trace logging on pasta

> Next things to check:
> 
> * Can we get a full log, or at least a section leading up to the `Dropping datagram` message.  There might be other messages giving more detail about why we're failing.

@yuki this would be something like `--net=pasta:--debug,--log-file,/tmp/pasta.log,--log-size,1000000000` passed in Podman's command line.

> * Especially if we could enable debug or trace logging on pasta

...and `--trace` instead of `--debug` if you can still reproduce the issue and use qbittorrent like that (it will slow things down massively).

This snippet is enough to get us one step further.  We're getting the multicast packet from the container, and attempting to create a flow for it.  That fails due to an `EADDRNOTAVAIL` on `connect(2)`.  So, we fail to create the flow and therefore drop the packet.  That appears to happen every time the guest tries to send this multicast, which could then explain the failures in the server itself.  Next steps of debugging this specific failure in the next comment, but a few general observations for now.

- These packets don't appear to be especially frequent: bursts of 3 packets, 2-4 seconds apart, and eventually stopping.
- That means dropping the packets is not enough to explain the high CPU load.  To clarify, the load was on the pasta process itself, not the qtbittorrent container?
- As noted, this appears to be bittorrent local peer discovery.  I don't know exactly how that works, but presumably the server both sends out these multicast announcements and listens to similar announcements from other nearby peers.  While I think it won't be to hard to fix things so that the multicast announcement makes it out, letting the multicasts back *in* is much harder (pasta will need to snoop and process IGMP and MLD traffic).  We'd like to do this, but it's giong to be a pretty big endeavour (this is upstream [bug 2](https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=2).
- Since even if we fix the immediate problem, local peer discovery is unlikely to work, disabling it could be a good workaround for the time being.

@yuki
> I have updated the Qbittorrent image (lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent) and all the services that I have running, and now I'm also having IPv4 drops in the logs
> 
> ```
> jun 03 20:57:00 archy pasta[202587]: Can't process IPv4 fragments (13 dropped)
> jun 03 20:57:00 archy pasta[202587]: Can't process IPv4 fragments (14 dropped)
> ```

This is unlikely to be related to the IPv6 bug here, could you file a separate issue for it to keep the discussion more coherent (short answer is pasta doesn't support fragments, if the guest is sending them, that probably indicates a problem with MTU configuration).

Now investigating the cause of the specific error:
```
0.2843:          Flow 25 (UDP flow): Couldn't connect flow socket: Cannot assign requested address
```

(Aside: now that have proper ratelimiting, this should be promoted to a warning, not debug message; I'll work on a patch for that)

This message indicates that when we tried to `connect(2)` to the multicast address, we got an `EADDRNOTAVAIL` error.  According to the man page this means:
```
       EADDRNOTAVAIL
              (Internet domain sockets) The socket referred to by sockfd had not previously been bound to an address and, upon attempting to bind it to an ephemeral port, it was determined that all port numbers in the ephemeral port range are currently in use.  See the  discussion
              of /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range in ip(7).
```

Which is weird, because in this case we're *not* looking for an ephemeral port - we're specifically binding to source port 6771, or at least we should be.  It's possible something is wrong in our code for this, but I think that's unlikely - running out of ephemeral ports also seems very unlikely.  My guess is that the man page is not quite correct and there are also other circumstances which can cause an `EADDRNOTAVAIL` error.  Possibly a conflict with something else running on the host, although I'd expect that to show up at `bind(2)` time rather than `connect(2)` time.  It's also possible there's some multicast specific behaviour going on here.

- @dgibson: Make that error and maybe some ones like it appear without `--debug`
- @dgibson: Check behaviour of `connect()` with multicast addresses to see if I can reproduce something like this

[Note to self, and maybe @sbrivio-rh]

Haven't checked thoroughly, but [this line](https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv6/addrconf.c#n1903) in the kernel may be relevant.  I _think_ that function is choosing an IPv6 source address where we haven't specified one by `bind()`ing the socket - which is the situation we're in here.  That line appears to be generating an `EADDRNOTAVAIL` under circumstances I haven't yet figured out.

> I _think_ that function is choosing an IPv6 source address where we haven't specified one by `bind()`ing the socket - which is the situation we're in here.

Right, yes. That function implements a part of the rules from RFC 6724 Section 5 (the comment to "Section 4" is slightly misleading), https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6724#section-5.

> That line appears to be generating an `EADDRNOTAVAIL` under circumstances I haven't yet figured out.

I think it helps looking at that function before kernel commit a9b05723ffa2 ("[IPV6] ADDRCONF: Clean-up ipv6_dev_get_saddr()."), or even at that commit itself. It doesn't really introduce any functional change but it makes things much harder to understand (at least for myself). That EADDRNOTAVAIL simply means that those rules (now mostly evaluated by ipv6_get_saddr_eval()) didn't yield any usable address, which might be the case for multicast, even though rule 2. says "**Prefer** appropriate scope", not _require_ or suchlike.

Maybe we should bind to an address explicitly in this case.

> Maybe we should bind to an address explicitly in this case.

Oops, sorry, I lost track of the actual issue... I guess we should try to avoid connect()ing to multicast instead.

I suspect the load issue is unrelated to the dropped datagram issue, but I'm much less confident.  Obviously I can't be certain until we figure the root cause for at least one of them.  Although it's not necessarily the worst, I'm still planning to pursue the datagram dropping one for now, we have the clearest leads.

> Can I get the log in journalctl and also in `--log-file` when adding `--trace` ? 

No, I'm afraid not.  At present pasta logs to a file *or* to the system log, never both.  We could in principle add support for it, but we're unlikely to get around to implementing it any time soon.  If you want to log to journalctl instead of the logfile, you can drop `--log-file` while keeping `--trace`.  Usually we find a discrete log file is more convenient when using detailed log levels, but the option is there.

> Will I see something if I do `tail -f /tmp/pasta-trace.log | grep -i dropping` as it appears in journalctl?

Sorry, I don't really understand the question.  Apart from the prefixes added by the system logger, the messages are identical whether they go to a log file or to journalctl, if that's what you mean.

I have configured /etc/containers/containers.conf and modified the helper_binaries_dir adding /usr/lib/podman and the directory with the new compilation.

Ok, thanks for the traces.  No new insights so far, alas.

A few extra notes that are consequences of things before, but that may not be immediately apparent:

It would be interesting (for both @yuki and myself, at least) to know the reason for the [test failure](https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/23739#issuecomment-4656630300).  However, whatever it is, it indicates a problem that can't be fixed within pasta:

- The test program is attempting essentially the same multicast that we want to send out.  If we can't do it directly from the host, then clearly pasta can't forward such a multicast from the guest.
- It might be possible to have pasta synthesise an ICMPv6 message back to the guest reporting the error.  qtbittorrent could, at least in theory detect that and have a positive indication of failure.  I kind of doubt that's actually going to do anything, since UDP messages are usually unreliable anyway.  It's also a moderately large amount of work (at least to generalise to other socket errors that might be reportable that way).

Suggested next steps on several fronts:

### Multicast address allocation failures (`Dropping datagram`)

It would still be good to know exactly what's going on here.

@yuki next steps would be:
-  `ip nexthop ls`
-  `ip route get ff15::efc0:988f`

@psyke83, @andsky-git that information, plus `ip -6 addr` and `ip -6 route` would also be useful from you, to see if there's a common factor causing the multicast failure.

### High CPU load

Based on the latest information, I'm now pretty confident this is not connected to the `Dropping datagram` errors.  Along with that, I think it's more likely to be related to the root cause of the actually failing to work.  Since much of the bug history here is tracking the dropped datagram problem, @yuki (or anyone who's seeing it) could you please file a new bug to track the high load problem?
